agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services. The CEO describes an automated system that adjusts delivery prices based on demand signals, with undefined guardrails to prevent extreme pricing. The spec is extremely vague — a verbal all-hands description, not a product requirements document.","bounded_context":"Pricing","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"DynaPricing","definition":"The product name for the real-time dynamic delivery pricing system"},{"term":"Demand","definition":"Undefined measure of delivery request volume relative to some baseline — no definition of how demand is measured, what signals compose it, or what constitutes high vs low"},{"term":"Guardrails","definition":"Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme pricing — CEO said 'probably', meaning not yet committed"},{"term":"Real-time","definition":"Prices adjust dynamically, not on a batch/scheduled basis — exact latency SLA undefined"},{"term":"Competitive","definition":"Prices should not drive customers to competitors — implies awareness of market rates, but no mechanism specified"},{"term":"Profitable","definition":"Prices must cover costs and contribute margin — implies a price floor at minimum, but no margin target defined"}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery prices must increase when demand is high relative to a baseline","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy — the direction of the price-demand correlation is a strategy choice, not a data integrity constraint. A VP could decide to absorb high demand at flat rates as a growth strategy.","testable_assertion":"Given demand above the defined 'high' threshold, the computed price must be greater than the base price"},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Delivery prices must decrease when demand is low relative to a baseline","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy to stimulate demand during slow periods. Management could choose to keep prices flat during low demand instead.","testable_assertion":"Given demand below the defined 'low' threshold, the computed price must be less than the base price"},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Price adjustments must occur in real time, not via batch or manual intervention","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Automation is a design goal, not a data integrity constraint. The system could fall back to manual pricing without corrupting state.","testable_assertion":"When a demand signal changes, the effective delivery price must update without human intervention and within a defined latency SLA (SLA TBD)"},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"An upper bound (price ceiling) must exist to prevent prices from exceeding a maximum","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Even though the CEO said 'probably,' a price ceiling is a physical/legal/reputational constraint. Unbounded pricing can violate consumer protection laws, trigger regulatory action, and cause irreversible brand damage. This is a system integrity invariant — the system must never produce a price above a defined maximum.","testable_assertion":"No computed price may ever exceed the configured maximum price ceiling, regardless of demand level"},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"A lower bound (price floor) must exist to prevent prices from falling below cost","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Pricing below cost is a financial integrity constraint. Every delivery has a real cost (fuel, labor, vehicle). Pricing below that cost means guaranteed losses on every transaction — this is not a policy preference, it is a solvency constraint.","testable_assertion":"No computed price may ever be less than the configured minimum price floor"},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"The pricing function must be deterministic for a given set of inputs at a point in time","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Two identical delivery requests at the same moment with the same demand signal must receive the same price. Non-deterministic pricing would violate fairness expectations and create audit/reconciliation failures — this is data consistency.","testable_assertion":"Given identical demand inputs and configuration, the pricing function must return the same price"},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"Price changes must be auditable — every price computation must be traceable to the demand signal and parameters that produced it","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Auditability is a data integrity constraint. Without it, the system cannot be debugged, audited for regulatory compliance, or defended in a dispute. Once a price is served, the reasoning must be reconstructable.","testable_assertion":"Every price served must have a corresponding audit record containing the demand signal value, pricing parameters, timestamp, and resulting price"}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Demand signal spikes instantaneously (e.g., concert ends, weather event) causing a massive price jump within seconds","expected_behavior":"Price must increase but must not exceed the ceiling. Rate-of-change limiting may be needed but is not specified."},{"scenario":"Demand drops to zero (e.g., holiday, outage in a zone)","expected_behavior":"Price must decrease but must not fall below the floor. System must handle zero-demand gracefully without division-by-zero or undefined behavior."},{"scenario":"Two customers request delivery at the same instant in the same zone","expected_behavior":"Both must receive the same price for equivalent deliveries — determinism and fairness invariant applies."},{"scenario":"Guardrail configuration is missing or corrupted at startup","expected_behavior":"System must fail closed — refuse to serve prices rather than serve unbounded prices. This is a safety-critical default."},{"scenario":"Competitor prices are dramatically lower during a high-demand period","expected_behavior":"Undefined. The CEO mentioned 'competitive' but no mechanism exists to incorporate competitor pricing. Risk of losing customers to competitors during surge."},{"scenario":"Rapid oscillation between high and low demand (flapping)","expected_behavior":"Undefined. Without smoothing or hysteresis, prices could oscillate wildly, confusing customers and creating arbitrage opportunities."},{"scenario":"The system is fully automated but produces obviously wrong prices (e.g., $0.01 or $10,000)","expected_behavior":"Guardrails (floor/ceiling) should catch extremes, but there is no human-in-the-loop override mechanism specified for edge cases the guardrails miss."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Direct revenue impact — dynamic pricing can significantly increase revenue during peak demand (industry benchmarks: 15-30% revenue uplift from surge pricing). However, incorrect implementation risks underpricing (margin erosion) or overpricing (demand destruction). The floor/ceiling guardrails are the critical financial safety net.","operational":"Fully automated pricing removes manual intervention but introduces operational complexity: monitoring demand signals, maintaining pricing algorithms, tuning parameters, and responding to anomalies. Requires real-time infrastructure with high availability — pricing downtime means no deliveries or fallback to stale prices.","user":"Customers will experience variable pricing, which is a significant UX and trust concern. Surge pricing is widely understood (Uber precedent) but also widely disliked. Transparency about why prices change is not addressed. Customer communication strategy is entirely missing from the spec.","risk":"HIGH risk due to spec vagueness. Key risks: (1) Regulatory — many jurisdictions have price gouging laws, especially during emergencies; (2) Reputational — surge pricing during crises (natural disasters, pandemics) causes severe backlash; (3) Technical — real-time pricing at scale is complex; failures could serve wrong prices; (4) Competitive — if guardrails are too loose, customers flee; if too tight, the system adds complexity without value.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Will see variable delivery prices. Trust and transparency are critical. No communication strategy defined."},{"group":"Delivery Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Unclear if courier compensation changes with dynamic pricing. If prices go up but courier pay stays flat, this creates labor tension. If pay scales with price, cost structure changes."},{"group":"Finance/Revenue Team","impact":"Must define floor, ceiling, and margin targets. Currently no numbers exist in the spec."},{"group":"Operations/Dispatch","impact":"Must provide or integrate demand signals. Definition of 'demand' is their responsibility to operationalize."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Must review for price gouging laws, consumer protection, and regulatory requirements by jurisdiction."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will receive complaints about price changes. Need scripts, policies, and tools to explain and potentially override dynamic prices."},{"group":"Competitors","impact":"CEO mentioned competitiveness but defined no mechanism. Competitive intelligence may need to feed into the system."}]},"confidence":{"level":30,"reasoning":"This is a verbal CEO statement at an all-hands meeting, not a product specification. Almost every critical parameter is undefined: what constitutes 'high' or 'low' demand, what the demand signal is, what the floor and ceiling values are, what 'real-time' means in terms of latency, what 'competitive' means operationally, and whether guardrails are even committed ('probably'). The only high-confidence extractions are the directional relationship between demand and price and the need for some form of bounds. Everything else requires significant product definition work before implementation.","high_confidence_areas":["Price increases when demand is high (explicitly stated)","Price decreases when demand is low (explicitly stated)","System is automated (explicitly stated)","Some form of guardrails will exist (stated with hedge)"],"low_confidence_areas":["Definition of demand signal (not specified)","Thresholds for high vs low demand (not specified)","Specific floor and ceiling values (not specified)","Latency SLA for real-time (not specified)","How competitiveness is measured or enforced (not specified)","Whether courier pay is affected (not addressed)","Rate-of-change limiting or smoothing (not addressed)","Emergency/crisis pricing policy (not addressed)","Customer transparency and communication (not addressed)","Jurisdictional regulatory compliance (not addressed)"],"assumptions":["A price floor at or above cost is a hard requirement even though the CEO did not explicitly state it — this is a financial solvency invariant","A price ceiling exists — the CEO hedged with 'probably' but unbounded pricing is not viable for legal and reputational reasons","Deterministic pricing for identical inputs is assumed as a fairness requirement","Audit trail is assumed as a regulatory and operational necessity"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Demand' is undefined. Is it request volume per zone per time window? Is it ratio of requests to available couriers? Is it a composite signal? The entire pricing model depends on this definition and it is completely absent.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations/Dispatch","Finance","Engineering"],"options":["Define demand as raw request count per zone per time window","Define demand as request-to-courier ratio (supply/demand)","Define demand as a composite signal including time-of-day, zone, weather, events"],"recommendation":"Request-to-courier ratio is the most meaningful signal in logistics — it captures both demand AND supply constraints. Recommend product team define this formally before any implementation."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Guardrails' are uncommitted ('probably') and completely undefined. No floor value, no ceiling value, no rate-of-change limits, no emergency policies.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance","Finance","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["Define static floor/ceiling as multiples of base price (e.g., 0.8x to 3.0x)","Define dynamic floor/ceiling based on cost-plus-margin model","Define tiered guardrails by jurisdiction to comply with local price gouging laws"],"recommendation":"At minimum, define a hard floor at cost-plus-minimum-margin and a hard ceiling as a multiple of base price. Jurisdictional overrides should be a day-one requirement given price gouging law variability."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Competitive' is stated as a goal but no mechanism is defined to measure or enforce competitiveness. Is this a soft aspiration or a hard constraint?","affected_stakeholders":["Product","Finance","Customers"],"options":["Ignore competitor pricing — optimize only on internal demand signals","Incorporate competitor price feeds as a ceiling constraint","Use competitor pricing as one input to the demand model"],"recommendation":"Clarify with CEO whether 'competitive' is aspirational or requires active competitor price monitoring. If active, this is a significant scope addition."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No mention of how dynamic pricing affects courier/driver compensation. If delivery price doubles but courier pay is flat, this creates labor relations risk. If courier pay scales, the cost model changes fundamentally.","affected_stakeholders":["Couriers/Drivers","HR/Labor Relations","Finance"],"options":["Courier pay is decoupled from dynamic pricing","Courier pay includes a dynamic component tied to surge pricing","Courier pay uses a separate incentive model during high demand"],"recommendation":"This must be defined before launch. Industry precedent (Uber, DoorDash) shows courier pay during surge is a critical retention and fairness issue."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No regulatory or legal review mentioned. Dynamic pricing in delivery services may be subject to price gouging statutes in many jurisdictions, especially during declared emergencies.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance","Executive Team"],"options":["Proceed with legal review as a parallel workstream","Block implementation until legal review is complete"],"recommendation":"Legal review must happen in parallel. At minimum, implement a kill switch to disable dynamic pricing per jurisdiction in case of regulatory challenge."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
